name: LuaJIT IR
summary: Mike Pall's SSA IR for LuaJIT: trace-shaped — a linear sequence of operations recorded through one iteration of a hot loop, with guards on every speculation and side-exits to the interpreter. Demonstrates that SSA can be maximally compact when scoped to a single hot path.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
LuaJIT's IR (Mike Pall's design) is SSA-form but *trace-shaped*: a trace records the linear sequence of operations executed through one iteration of a hot loop, including inlined function calls. Guards check that every speculation (type, branch, allocation) holds; if a guard fails, execution falls back to the interpreter at a side exit, which may start recording a new trace.

The IR is dense, with carefully-tuned representation: each op is a fixed-size record, with operands as small indices into the trace buffer. Allocation sinking, store forwarding, and constant folding all operate on the trace as a linear sequence.

sections.title: Trace-form vs method-form SSA
sections.content:
Where method-JIT SSA IRs (LLVM, Cranelift, Turboshaft, sea-of-nodes) compile entire functions, trace IRs compile linearised hot paths. The benefit is that branch-heavy interpreter dispatch loops collapse into a straight-line trace through one observed control flow, ideal for languages with heavy dynamic dispatch and tagged-value boxing.

The cost is trace explosion: pathological branchy code can produce hundreds of side-exit-spawned traces, each with overlapping content. LuaJIT mitigates with side-trace abandonment heuristics and trace-recording quotas.

sections.title: Production-quality compactness
sections.content:
LuaJIT's trace compiler is the cleanest production demonstration that an SSA IR can be maximally compact when scoped to a single hot path. Fixed-size op records, dense small-integer operand indices, and fused recording-plus-optimisation make the IR small enough to fit in cache while the trace is being constructed.

The design pattern transfers to any language whose dynamic-dispatch overhead outweighs typical static-language compile-time tradeoffs: trace IRs are an option whenever method-JITs cannot amortise their compile cost over short-lived hot loops.
